title: Retain or Transfer?
subtitle: Deductibles, self-insurance & captives

hook.metaphor: You don't claim on your car insurance for a ₹500 scratch — the hassle and lost no-claims bonus aren't worth it, so you just pay it yourself. You insure the big crash, not the scratch. Every organisation makes this same call at scale: which risks to *keep* (retain) and which to *hand off* (transfer). Insure everything and you're overpaying; insure nothing and one big hit ruins you. The art is the line between.
hook.scenario: A large company insures every tiny risk — even ₹2,000 breakages — paying hefty premiums and admin for each. A consultant points out it's paying far more in premiums than it ever gets back on the small stuff. What should it do differently?

learn: The fundamental choice is **retain vs transfer.** *Retain* = accept and budget for a risk yourself; *transfer* = pay an insurer to carry it. The guiding rule: **transfer what could be ruinous, retain what you can comfortably absorb.** Our company has it backwards — insuring trivial breakages it could easily self-fund, paying premium-plus-admin for the privilege.

Why retain small risks? Because insurance isn't free: every policy carries the insurer's costs and profit margin on top of the expected loss. For small, frequent, predictable losses, it's almost always cheaper to **pay them yourself** than to pay someone else to pay them (the consultant's point).

The everyday tool for blending the two is the **deductible/excess**: you retain the first slice of each loss and transfer the rest. A *higher* deductible means you retain more, so your premium drops — a deliberate dial between retaining and transferring.

At scale, organisations formalise retention with **self-insurance** (deliberately setting aside funds to cover expected losses) and even **captives** (a company's own in-house insurance subsidiary). These make sense when you're big enough to pool your *own* risks and want to cut out the external insurer's margin.

The decision ties back to **risk appetite** (the recurring anchor): how much loss can we comfortably absorb ourselves? Below that line, retain (cheaper); above it — the potentially ruinous — transfer (worth the premium). Insuring everything is as much a failure of judgement as insuring nothing.
bridge: So at work, you can make the insurance decision deliberately instead of by default: *which risks could ruin us (transfer those) and which can we comfortably absorb (retain those, and stop paying premium-plus-margin to insure them)?* Use deductibles to tune the split, consider self-insurance for predictable small losses at scale, and anchor it all to how much you can afford to lose — your risk appetite.

interactions.title: Insuring the ₹2,000 breakages
interactions.prompt: A company insures even tiny breakages, paying more in premiums than it recovers. What should it do?

options.label: Keep insuring everything — more cover is always safer.
options.feedback: Insuring trivial, affordable losses means paying premium-plus-margin to cover what you could easily self-fund. That's overpaying, not safety — insuring everything is a judgement failure too.

options.label: Retain the small, affordable risks (raise deductibles) and insure only the ruinous ones.
options.feedback: Exactly. Transfer what could ruin you; retain what you can comfortably absorb. Higher deductibles or self-insuring the small stuff cuts wasted premium while keeping protection where it matters.

options.label: Drop all insurance to save money.
options.feedback: The opposite error — now one big fire or lawsuit could wipe it out. You retain the small and transfer the ruinous, not abandon cover entirely.

interactions.title: Retain or transfer?
interactions.prompt: Sort each risk by the smarter default response.

items.label: Small everyday losses you can easily absorb

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Transfer what could ruin you, retain what you can comfortably absorb — you insure the big crash, not the ₹500 scratch — using deductibles and self-insurance to tune the split against your risk appetite.”

checkpoint.q: The guiding rule for retain vs transfer is…
options: Transfer the potentially ruinous; retain what you can comfortably absorb
checkpoint.explain: Insure the big crash, self-fund the small scratch — both insuring everything and nothing are mistakes.

options: Lowers your premium, because you retain more of each loss
checkpoint.explain: A higher deductible means you keep more of the risk, so the insurer charges less to carry the rest.

checkpoint.q: Why is it often cheaper to self-fund small, frequent losses?
options: Insurers pay you to do so
Insurance adds the insurer's costs and margin on top of the expected loss
checkpoint.explain: For predictable small losses, paying premium-plus-margin to an insurer costs more than just paying them yourself.
